sections.title: The Subjunctive with Να
sections.explanation:
Greek has no infinitive. Instead, the subjunctive mood (υποτακτική) is used with the particle να wherever English would use 'to + verb'. Να is invariable — it never changes form — and it is always followed by a conjugated verb.

WHEN TO USE ΝΑ + SUBJUNCTIVE:
After modal and control verbs: θέλω (I want), μπορώ (I can / am able), πρέπει (it is necessary / must), αρέσει (it pleases / I like), αρχίζω (I begin), προσπαθώ (I try), φοβάμαι (I am afraid).

TWO ASPECTS — IMPERFECTIVE vs. PERFECTIVE:
The aspect of the verb after να changes the meaning:

  IMPERFECTIVE (present stem) — ongoing or repeated action:
    Θέλω να τρώω. = I want to be eating. / I want to eat (regularly).

  PERFECTIVE (aorist stem, no augment) — single, complete action:
    Θέλω να φάω. = I want to eat (now / once).

In most everyday contexts the perfective is the neutral, default choice. The imperfective after να emphasises repetition or continuity.

CONJUGATION EXAMPLE — perfective subjunctive of γράφω:
  να γράψω  (that I write)
  να γράψεις (that you write)
  να γράψει  (that he/she writes)
  να γράψουμε (that we write)
  να γράψετε  (that you all write)
  να γράψουν  (that they write)

Notice: same endings as the present tense, but built on the aorist stem and without the augment ε-.
sections.tip: A quick test: if the English sentence has 'to + verb' after words like want, can, must, like — Greek will almost always use να + subjunctive. There is no Greek word for the English 'to' before a verb.

examples.en: I want to eat something tasty. (perfective — a single act of eating)

examples.el: Θέλω να τρώω καλά κάθε μέρα.
examples.en: I want to eat well every day. (imperfective — ongoing / habitual)
I like to read books. (imperfective — general habit)
I am trying to learn Greek.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I want to go to Athens' in Greek?
exercises.explanation: Greek requires να between θέλω and the verb. 'Θέλω να πάω' = I want to go. The particle να cannot be omitted, and ότι introduces statements of fact, not wishes.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence expresses 'I want to eat' as a single action right now (perfective)?
exercises.explanation: 'Να φάω' uses the perfective (aorist) stem φα- and expresses a single, complete act of eating. 'Να τρώω' uses the present (imperfective) stem and implies ongoing or habitual eating.

exercises.prompt: What role does the particle να play in Greek grammar?
options: It marks the future tense, like θα.
It introduces the subjunctive and replaces the infinitive ('to + verb').
exercises.explanation: Να is the subjunctive particle. Greek has no infinitive, so να + conjugated verb does the job of English 'to + verb'. It is different from θα (future) and δεν/μην (negation).

sections.title: Subjunctive After Conjunctions
sections.explanation:
The subjunctive (να + verb) is used after a range of conjunctions expressing purpose, time, and manner. These conjunctions are very common in everyday speech.

PURPOSE — 'in order to':
  για να + subjunctive
  Φεύγω νωρίς για να προλάβω το λεωφορείο.
  (I'm leaving early in order to catch the bus.)

BEFORE — 'before':
  πριν (να) + subjunctive
  Τηλεφώνησέ μου πριν έρθεις.
  (Call me before you come.)

WITHOUT — 'without':
  χωρίς να + subjunctive
  Έφυγε χωρίς να πει αντίο.
  (He left without saying goodbye.)

TIME CONJUNCTIONS — these take the subjunctive when referring to a future or anticipated event:
  αφού (after)     — Αφού φας, μπορείς να παίξεις. (After you eat, you can play.)
  μόλις (as soon as) — Μόλις φτάσεις, τηλεφώνησέ μου. (As soon as you arrive, call me.)
  όταν (when)      — Όταν μεγαλώσεις, θα καταλάβεις. (When you grow up, you will understand.)

Note: αφού, μόλις, and όταν do not always take να in the clause itself (the verb after them is directly in the subjunctive form), but they trigger the subjunctive/perfective verb form when the event is anticipated rather than factual.
sections.tip: Για να is the clearest purpose marker — whenever you want to say 'in order to' or simply 'to' (with a purpose), use για να + subjunctive. Χωρίς να always needs να, even when English just says 'without doing'.

examples.el: Σπουδάζω πολύ για να περάσω τις εξετάσεις.
examples.en: I study a lot in order to pass the exams.

examples.el: Πήγαμε νωρίς για να βρούμε καλές θέσεις.
examples.en: We went early in order to find good seats.
You must finish before we leave.

examples.el: Βγήκε χωρίς να πάρει τα κλειδιά του.
examples.en: He went out without taking his keys.

examples.el: Μόλις φτάσεις στο σταθμό, στείλε μου μήνυμα.
examples.en: As soon as you arrive at the station, send me a message.

examples.el: Όταν τελειώσεις, έλα να με βρεις.
examples.en: When you finish, come and find me.

exercises.prompt: Which conjunction expresses purpose ('in order to') and requires να + subjunctive?
exercises.explanation: 'Για να' specifically marks purpose: 'I did X in order to achieve Y'. Both χωρίς να and πριν να also use the subjunctive, but they express 'without' and 'before' respectively, not purpose.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'She left without eating'?
exercises.explanation: 'Χωρίς να' means 'without'. 'Έφυγε χωρίς να φάει' = she left without eating. 'Πριν να φάει' = before eating. 'Για να φάει' = in order to eat.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'μόλις' (as soon as) with the subjunctive?
options: Μόλις να έρθεις, θα σου πω τα νέα.
Μόλις ότι έρθεις, θα σου πω τα νέα.
Μόλις έρθεις, θα σου πω τα νέα.
exercises.explanation: 'Μόλις' is followed directly by the verb in subjunctive form without an additional να. 'Μόλις έρθεις, θα σου πω τα νέα' = As soon as you come, I will tell you the news.

sections.title: The Imperative: Commands and Requests
sections.explanation:
The imperative (προστακτική) is used to give commands, instructions, and requests. Like the subjunctive, Greek imperatives come in two aspects.

IMPERFECTIVE IMPERATIVE (ongoing or repeated action):
Take the 2nd person singular present tense and drop the final -ς.
  μιλάς → Μίλα!  (Speak! / Keep talking!)
  γράφεις → Γράφε! (Write! / Keep writing!)
  τρώς → Τρώε!    (Eat! / Keep eating!)

For the plural (you all), use the regular 2nd person plural present:
  Μιλάτε!  (Speak, everyone! / You all speak!)
  Γράφετε! (Write, everyone!)

PERFECTIVE IMPERATIVE (single, one-off action):
Built on the aorist stem (same stem as the perfective subjunctive, no augment).
Singular endings vary: many verbs take -ε, -ς, or -άσε/-ήσε.
  Γράψε! (Write! — one specific thing)
  Πές μου! (Tell me!)
  Πήγαινε! (Go!)

NEGATIVE IMPERATIVE:
Use μην + subjunctive form.
  Μην μιλάς! (Don't speak! — imperfective)
  Μην τρως! (Don't eat! — imperfective)
  Μην ξεχάσεις! (Don't forget! — perfective, specific one-time action)

Note: the negative imperative always uses μην (not δεν). It typically takes the imperfective (present) form for general or ongoing prohibitions, but the perfective is also used, especially for specific one-time actions (e.g. Μην ξεχάσεις τα κλειδιά σου! — Don't forget your keys!).

POLITENESS:
For polite requests, the subjunctive with να or a question with μπορείς/μπορείτε is preferred over the bare imperative:
  Μπορείτε να μου φέρετε νερό; (Could you bring me some water?)
sections.tip: Remember: μην is for imperatives and subjunctives (prohibitions and wishes); δεν is for statements of fact. Never use δεν to negate a command.

examples.en: Speak more quietly, please. (imperfective imperative — manner of speaking)
Write your name here. (perfective imperative — single specific action)
Don't speak with your mouth full. (negative imperative)
Don't forget your keys. (negative imperative — perfective subjunctive form)

exercises.prompt: How do you form the imperfective imperative singular from the verb 'μιλάς' (you speak)?
exercises.explanation: The imperfective imperative singular is formed by dropping the -ς from the 2nd person present: μιλάς → Μίλα! 'Μίλησε' is actually the perfective imperative (single action).

exercises.prompt: Which word is used to form a negative imperative (prohibition)?
exercises.explanation: Negative imperatives use 'μην', not 'δεν'. 'Δεν' negates statements (e.g. δεν μιλάω = I don't speak). 'Μην' negates commands and subjunctives (μην μιλάς = don't speak).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence is a correct negative command meaning 'Don't forget'?
exercises.explanation: 'Μην ξεχάσεις' is the correct negative imperative: μην + perfective subjunctive form. 'Δεν ξεχνάς' is a present-tense statement ('you don't forget'), not a command. 'Μη να' is not a valid combination.
